sourceTitle: Notion for creative agencies: managing internal and client work

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of creating a top‑level page for each client in Notion?

options.text: To store the client’s personal contact information only
options.explanation: Top‑level pages hold all client‑related content, not just contacts.

options.text: To act as a central homepage where all client‑specific information and sub‑pages are nested
options.explanation: Correct – the client top‑level page serves as the hub for everything related to that client.

options.text: To give the client admin rights over the entire agency workspace
options.explanation: Clients should only see their own pages, not the whole agency workspace.

options.text: To replace the agency’s internal wiki entirely
options.explanation: The client page complements, not replaces, the internal agency wiki.

questions.question: When sharing a client page with a guest, which permission level is most appropriate for typical client collaboration?

options.text: Can edit and delete any content
options.explanation: Clients usually should not edit agency‑created content.

options.text: Can view and leave comments but not edit the page
options.explanation: Correct – view + comment lets clients give feedback without altering the source.

options.text: Can only view the page with no commenting ability
options.explanation: Commenting is essential for feedback loops.

options.text: Can share the page with other external users
options.explanation: Guests typically cannot re‑share unless given explicit permission.

questions.question: Which Notion database view would be most helpful for quickly spotting upcoming project deadlines?

options.text: Board view
options.explanation: Board view shows status columns but not a chronological list.

options.text: Calendar view
options.explanation: Calendar view displays date properties, making deadlines easy to spot.

options.text: Gallery view
options.explanation: Gallery view is visual and not focused on dates.

options.text: List view
options.explanation: List view can show dates but isn’t as visual as a calendar for deadlines.

questions.question: Why might an agency create multiple database templates for a client instead of a single generic template?

options.text: To ensure each project type follows a tailored set of prompts and standards
options.explanation: Templates can embed process‑specific fields, keeping work consistent for each project type.

options.text: To increase the number of pages in the workspace for SEO purposes
options.explanation: Notion page count does not affect SEO; this is not a reason.

options.text: To prevent team members from creating new pages
options.explanation: Templates aid creation, they don’t restrict it.

options.text: To make the workspace look more complex and impressive to clients
options.explanation: Complexity without purpose can hinder usability.

questions.question: Which property combination best supports tracking who is responsible for a project and who needs to review it?

options.text: Select property for status and Date property for deadline
options.explanation: These track progress and timing, not people.

options.text: Person property named “Project owner” and an additional Person property for reviewers
options.explanation: Correct – Person properties let you assign owners and reviewers.

options.text: Number property for budget and Formula property for cost calculations
options.explanation: Budget info is useful but not about responsibility.

options.text: Multi‑select property for project tags and URL property for reference links
options.explanation: Tags and URLs help categorization, not ownership.

questions.question: How does using a single Notion page for both the agency’s work and client feedback reduce context switching?

options.text: It forces all communication to happen via email, which is faster
options.explanation: Email adds another tool, increasing context switching.

options.text: All project assets, comments, and documentation live together, so users don’t need to jump between apps
options.explanation: Correct – consolidating everything in one page keeps focus.

options.text: It automatically archives old versions of files
options.explanation: Archiving is unrelated to context switching.

options.text: It disables notifications from other tools
options.explanation: Notion doesn’t control external notifications.

questions.question: When adding a new property to a client project database, why might you choose a ‘Select’ property for the “Status” field instead of a ‘Text’ property?

options.text: Select properties limit entries to predefined options, ensuring consistent status naming
options.explanation: Predefined options prevent spelling variations and keep status uniform.

options.text: Select properties automatically calculate the project's progress percentage
options.explanation: Only formulas can calculate percentages.

options.text: Select properties can be edited by anyone, while Text cannot
options.explanation: Both can be edited based on permissions.

options.text: Select properties are hidden from guests, keeping them unaware of status
options.explanation: Visibility depends on page permissions, not property type.

questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes the difference between page comments and block comments?

options.text: Page comments can only be added by agency members, while block comments can be added by anyone
options.explanation: Both comment types respect page permissions; not about user type.

options.text: Page comments appear at the top of the page for general feedback, while block comments attach to a specific block for targeted feedback
options.explanation: Correct – this distinction defines their use cases.

options.text: Block comments are visible to all workspace members, whereas page comments are private to the author
options.explanation: Both are visible according to sharing settings.

options.text: Page comments automatically create a task in the project database, but block comments do not
options.explanation: Comments do not auto‑create tasks.

questions.question: An agency wants to embed a live Figma prototype inside a Notion project page. Which step is essential before the embed will work for both internal team members and the client?

options.text: Convert the Figma file to a PDF and upload it
options.explanation: PDF loses interactivity; embeds require a shareable link.

options.text: Set the Figma file’s sharing permissions to allow anyone with the link to view
options.explanation: Correct – the embed needs a publicly accessible link for all viewers.

options.text: Enable Notion’s beta embed feature in workspace settings
options.explanation: Embedding Figma does not require a beta flag.

options.text: Create a separate Notion database for design assets and link the prototype from there
options.explanation: Linking is optional; the key is the Figma sharing setting.

questions.question: Why might an agency prefer presenting directly from a Notion page rather than exporting a static PowerPoint deck to a client?

options.text: Notion presentations automatically translate into multiple languages
options.explanation: Notion does not provide automatic translation.

options.text: Clients can view and comment on live content, allowing real‑time feedback and note‑taking within the same document
options.explanation: Correct – live collaboration reduces hand‑offs and keeps feedback contextual.

options.text: Notion pages are immune to internet connectivity issues
options.explanation: Both require internet; Notion still depends on a connection.

options.text: Exported PowerPoint decks cannot include embedded media, whereas Notion can
options.explanation: PowerPoint can embed media; the advantage is interactivity, not media support.
